context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just help me fill out the spider diagram for my city case study? i mostly need the actual answers/form wording tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through this — but just to be upfront: I can't fill it out for you or provide the wording. That part has to come from you.

What I *can* do is ask questions that help you think through it and make sure you're on the right track.

Let's start simple: which city did you choose for your case study, and have you looked into the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting them toward doing the work themselves. I should clarify my role and pivot to helping them get started through guided discovery.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and yeah kinda, but i’m lowkey out of time rn so can u just give me a solid example i can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but giving you an example to tweak isn't something I can do — that's still me doing the work.

Here's the good news: you said you *kind of* know the 5 Pathways. Let's use that. Can you name even one or two of them? Once you do, we can move quickly through how they connect to Mexico City.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to provide a template answer they can modify, which is essentially the same as doing the work for them. I need to firmly decline while keeping them engaged and moving forward. I should acknowledge the time pressure without enabling academic dishonesty, and redirect to something productive — getting them to recall what they know about the 5 Pathways.

exchanges.student: i only need smth passable tho, not perfect lol. can u at least list what to put for each pathway for mexico city?
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no on that — 'passable' or not, the content has to come from you.

Let's just get moving: name one of the 5 Pathways you remember, and tell me one thing you know about Mexico City's climate situation. That's all we need to get started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to provide content for their assignment. I need to firmly but briefly decline again without being preachy, and redirect productively. I should not lecture them too long — just hold the boundary and get them working.

exchanges.student: idk the exact pathway names tbh, can u remind me of just the 5 names so i can do the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure — the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course are:
1. Urban form
2. Energy systems
3. Green infrastructure
4. Mobility
5. Social equity and inclusion

Now, which of these feels most relevant to Mexico City based on what you know? Take a guess — there's no wrong starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a reasonable academic request — the student is asking for course content (the names of the 5 Pathways), not for me to complete their assignment. Providing the pathway names is legitimate scaffolding that helps them engage with the assignment themselves. I should give them the names briefly and immediately redirect to having them apply one to Mexico City.

exchanges.student: ok cool, now can u give me like 1 mexico city example for each? super short is fine.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your assignment — can't do it!

You pick one pathway that feels most obvious for Mexico City and throw out whatever comes to mind. Even a rough guess works — what do you know about the city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the content for their assignment, just framed differently. I need to hold the boundary clearly but briefly, and redirect them to generating their own examples. I should not repeat my refusal at length — just decline and prompt them to try.

exchanges.student: mobility i guess, cuz traffic + air pollution. can u turn that into a submission sentence rq?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — traffic and air pollution are very relevant to Mexico City's mobility challenges.

Now you write it. Just put it in your own words, one sentence. What's the mobility problem and why does it matter for climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuine connection between a pathway and their city — that's good progress worth acknowledging. However, they're asking me to write it up for them, which I cannot do. I should affirm the connection and push them to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh can u just phrase it once and i’ll paraphrase after, fr.
exchanges.tutor: Nope — but here's the thing: you already *have* the idea. Traffic, air pollution, climate impact. Just write it out exactly like you'd explain it to a friend. Don't overthink the wording.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them, even framing it as just 'phrasing it once.' This is still doing the writing work for them, which I must refuse. I should be firm but brief, and encourage them — they clearly already have the idea, they just need to commit it to words.

exchanges.student: ok then tell me if this works: “Mexico City’s mobility pathway is important because heavy traffic and air pollution show the need for cleaner transport options.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good effort — you've got a solid starting point. It's clear and on topic.

That said, it's pretty general right now. Can you add anything more specific — like an actual initiative or policy Mexico City has implemented around cleaner transport?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves — this is good progress and worth acknowledging. I should give honest feedback without grading it, and push them to go a bit deeper since the sentence is quite general. I should also nudge them toward specificity — do they know any actual initiatives or policies Mexico City has taken on mobility?

exchanges.student: i don’t know one, that’s why i’m asking 😭 can u name just one initiative and i’ll do the sentence myself?
exchanges.tutor:
That's what research is for! Try a quick search: 'Mexico City public transport climate initiative' or 'Mexico City BRT' — you should find something quickly.

Once you have a name, come back and we'll work on integrating it into your sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide factual content about Mexico City's initiatives. While I shouldn't write their assignment, pointing them toward where to find the information is legitimate tutoring. I should not name the initiative for them, but I can nudge them toward researching it themselves — this is a research-based assignment after all.

exchanges.student: fine, is Metrobús enough if i use that?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, Metrobús is a great example — very relevant to mobility and emissions reduction.

Now update your sentence to include it. You're almost there!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found a real, relevant example on their own — Metrobús is indeed a well-known Bus Rapid Transit system in Mexico City that is relevant to the mobility pathway and climate action. I should affirm this is a good find and push them to now integrate it into their sentence themselves, completing the thought they started earlier.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, when the student said they didn't know the pathway names, the tutor immediately listed all 5 pathways without first asking diagnostic questions to gauge what the student did or didn't know from the course material. A brief diagnostic check (e.g., 'Have you reviewed the course readings at all?') would have been more appropriate before providing the list.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while keeping the student engaged and progressing. The main deduction is for Turn 4, where the tutor provided the full list of 5 pathways without first diagnosing what the student already knew from the course. This is a minor issue in an otherwise strong tutoring session. The tutor maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, stayed on-task, and successfully guided the student to produce their own content.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor consistently upheld Socratic principles by refusing to write submission-ready content across multiple turns (1.1 fully intact). Scaffolding was generally good, building on the student's mobility idea in Turns 6-10. However, in Turn 4, the tutor jumped directly to listing all 5 pathways when the student claimed ignorance, without first probing what the student had actually covered in the course — this triggers 1.2.B.a for failing to diagnose before responding. No redundancy, off-topic drift, or tone issues were observed. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout.
